metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC2's delivery ownership: either (a) add an explicit dependency edge on story ample-famous-cockatiel and change the AC to 'verify the iam.tf comment was updated in that story's merge commit' with a concrete grep proving command (`grep -v 'already exists in the account' infra/terraform/iam.tf`), or (b) remove the 'already applied' claim and own the change directly in this ticket.
findings.finding: AC2 (`infra/terraform/iam.tf` comment fix) is described in the 'What' section as 'already applied in the terraform story's commit', yet it appears as a standalone acceptance criterion in this ticket with no dependency edge to story ample-famous-cockatiel. An executor cannot tell whether (a) they must make the change here, (b) they must verify a sibling commit, or (c) the AC is already satisfied and merely needs attestation — and there is no stated proving command or cross-ticket dependency link.
findings.location: What section ('Fix the stale comment in `infra/terraform/iam.tf`') and Acceptance Criteria AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Add a dependency note: 'Depends on ample-famous-cockatiel merging first. AC2 proving command: `grep -c "already exists in the account" infra/terraform/iam.tf` must return 0.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `infra/terraform/iam.tf:136`'s stale 'already exists in the account' comment has been updated (or confirm the ample-famous-cockatiel commit landed it); treat the iam.tf comment fix as an open AC item in this story, not a pre-applied precondition.
findings.finding: The plan states the iam.tf OIDC comment fix was 'already applied in the terraform story's commit,' but the current repo file (infra/terraform/iam.tf:136) still contains the stale text 'The OIDC provider (token.actions.githubusercontent.com) already exists in the account.' The plan's scope section treats this as a pre-landed precondition rather than an open AC item.
findings.location: ## What — 'Fix the stale comment in `infra/terraform/iam.tf`... (already applied in the terraform story's commit)'; also AC: '`infra/terraform/iam.tf`'s OIDC comment no longer claims the provider is externally owned.'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove '(already applied in the terraform story's commit)' from the What section and keep the iam.tf comment fix as a full AC item to be done in this story's commit, since the file still carries the stale text.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the 'already applied in the terraform story's commit' parenthetical from the What section (or mark it as pending), since `infra/terraform/iam.tf:136` still carries the stale 'already exists in the account' comment — the fix has not yet landed.
findings.finding: The plan states the iam.tf stale comment was 'already applied in the terraform story's commit,' but `infra/terraform/iam.tf:136` still reads 'The OIDC provider (token.actions.githubusercontent.com) already exists in the account.' — the fix is NOT present.
findings.location: What section — 'Fix the stale comment in `infra/terraform/iam.tf`… (already applied in the terraform story's commit)'; also AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '(already applied in the terraform story's commit)' with '(to be applied as part of this ticket)' so the implementer knows the iam.tf edit is still required work.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the `infra/terraform/iam.tf` comment fix from the leaf's What section and AC list; it is owned and delivered by the parent terraform story (ample-famous-cockatiel) — the leaf may reference it as context/prerequisite but must not carry it as its own deliverable or AC.
findings.finding: The leaf claims `infra/terraform/iam.tf`'s stale OIDC comment as one of its four named artifacts (What section) and backs it with a dedicated AC, even though (a) the parent story (ample-famous-cockatiel) explicitly owns `infra/terraform/iam.tf` in its file_impact list and delivers that fix in its own commit, (b) the parent's delegated scope for this leaf is documentation only ("Docs are child 6725"), and (c) the leaf's own What section admits the fix was "already applied in the terraform story's commit." Including a terraform file as an AC in a docs-only leaf re-defines a deliverable the parent owns, creating ambiguous dual-ownership and blurring the leaf's declared scope boundary.
findings.location: Leaf plan: What bullet 2 ("Fix the stale comment in `infra/terraform/iam.tf`") and AC line 2 ("`infra/terraform/iam.tf`'s OIDC comment no longer claims the provider is externally owned")
findings.suggested_fix: Delete the second What bullet ("Fix the stale comment in `infra/terraform/iam.tf`…") and the second AC line ("`infra/terraform/iam.tf`'s OIDC comment no longer claims…") from the leaf. Add a brief note under Prerequisites or Context: "The stale iam.tf comment is resolved in the companion terraform story (ample-famous-cockatiel); this leaf verifies final prose only."

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the dep ticket (ample-famous-cockatiel) explicitly removes the 'already exists in the account' comment text from iam.tf line 136; if not, either remove the 'already applied' parenthetical and add an explicit iam.tf comment-edit step, or update the Scope section to acknowledge this as a Terraform-file edit (not purely documentation).
findings.finding: The plan claims in the 'What' section that the iam.tf comment fix was 'already applied in the terraform story's commit,' but the dependency ticket (ample-famous-cockatiel) only describes rewiring the ARN reference to `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider.github.arn` — it does not explicitly state the stale prose comment at line 136 ('The OIDC provider (token.actions.githubusercontent.com) already exists in the account.') is removed. The comment still exists at HEAD. If the dep ticket doesn't remove that comment text, AC-2 requires this ticket to edit iam.tf, contradicting the plan's 'Documentation only' scope claim.
findings.location: What section: 'Fix the stale comment in infra/terraform/iam.tf… (already applied in the terraform story's commit)'; Scope section: 'Documentation only'; AC-2: 'infra/terraform/iam.tf's OIDC comment no longer claims the provider is externally owned.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between Scope ('documentation only') and What ('already applied in the terraform story's commit') for the iam.tf comment: either remove it from this ticket's Scope + AC, make it an explicit verify-only step, or keep it as an owned deliverable with the 'already applied' note removed.
findings.finding: Scope declares 'Documentation only' and lists four artifacts, while What simultaneously says the iam.tf comment fix is 'already applied in the terraform story's commit' — yet AC2 still gates the ticket on that file. An implementer cannot determine whether (a) they must make this change, (b) it is pre-applied and they should merely verify, or (c) the Scope boundary excludes it entirely.
findings.location: Scope section; What bullet 2 ('Fix the stale comment in infra/terraform/iam.tf … already applied'); AC2
findings.suggested_fix: If the iam.tf change truly lands in the terraform story, remove it from this ticket's What/Scope/AC entirely and add a dependency note: 'AC2 is satisfied by story ample-famous-cockatiel; verify before closing.' If this ticket must own it, remove the 'already applied' parenthetical and keep AC2 as-is.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add 'cookie-secret idiom change' to the tradeoffs list in AC3 so the ADR's covering criterion matches its described content.
findings.finding: The ADR's described content in What includes 'cookie-secret idiom change' as a tradeoff to record, but AC3 lists only four tradeoffs (adopt-vs-teardown, import-not-recreate, name preservation, state migration) — the cookie-secret idiom change is absent from the acceptance criterion, leaving it uncovered.
findings.location: What section (ADR bullet); AC3
findings.suggested_fix: AC3: 'records the re-homing decision and its tradeoffs (adopt-vs-teardown, import-not-recreate, name preservation, cookie-secret idiom change, and state migration).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add 'cookie-secret idiom change' to the ADR acceptance criterion so the AC matches the What section's stated ADR scope.
findings.finding: The ADR Acceptance Criterion omits 'cookie-secret idiom change' from its required content, creating a gap between the What section and the AC checklist that could cause the ADR to be accepted without documenting this tradeoff.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — ADR bullet vs. What section (ADR item)
findings.suggested_fix: Update the AC bullet to: 'An ADR under `docs/adr/` records the re-homing decision and its tradeoffs (adopt-vs-teardown, import-not-recreate, name preservation, cookie-secret idiom change, state migration).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either commit snap's `auth/RUNBOOK.md` into a rebar archive path (e.g. `docs/archive/snap-auth-runbook.md`) before this ticket is worked, or replace the 'adapted from snap's `auth/RUNBOOK.md`' reference with a concrete outline of the required sections so the executor can produce the runbook without the external file.
findings.finding: The plan names `snap's auth/RUNBOOK.md` as the source document for adapting the SSO operations runbook, but this file does not exist anywhere in the rebar repo tree and no archived copy or path within rebar is cited. The file lives in a decommissioned external repo whose checkout location (`~/snap-oakhart-manual/...`) is an operator-local artifact not guaranteed to be available to any executor of this ticket.
findings.location: What section: 'Add an SSO operations runbook under infra/runbooks/sso-auth-host.md adapted from snap's `auth/RUNBOOK.md`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a step: 'Copy `~/snap-oakhart-manual/.../auth/RUNBOOK.md` into `docs/archive/snap-auth-runbook.md` as a source reference before starting this ticket' — or inline the key sections (architecture, secrets table, rotation commands) directly in the plan's What description.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that story ample-famous-cockatiel's Terraform work has actually landed an `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider` resource in `infra/terraform/` before writing the ADR and gerrit-aws-setup.md subsection asserting rebar owns it; if it has not landed, add a sequencing guard to this ticket's scope.
findings.finding: The plan asserts rebar now OWNS the GitHub OIDC provider (via story ample-famous-cockatiel), but no `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider` resource exists anywhere under `infra/terraform/` — the ownership that the ADR, the gerrit-aws-setup.md subsection, and AC1 all document is unverifiable in the codebase.
findings.location: Why/What sections — 'rebar now OWNS the GitHub OIDC provider'; AC1 and AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the intended ADR filename or naming convention (e.g., `docs/adr/NNNN-oidc-sso-rehoming.md` where NNNN is the next sequential number) so the executor does not have to infer it.
findings.finding: The ADR artifact has no specified filename or numbering convention, leaving the executor to infer it. While 'next sequential number in docs/adr/' is a common convention, if the repo uses a different scheme (e.g., date-based, topic-slug-only) this is a defaultable gap that could produce an incorrectly named file.
findings.location: What section: 'Add an ADR under `docs/adr/`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'Name the file `docs/adr/<next-sequential-number>-oidc-sso-rehoming.md` following the existing numeric prefix convention in that directory.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC with a concrete validation mechanism — e.g., 'a dry-run of the cookie-key rotation steps against the staging account confirms the SSM path names and Lambda invocation flag match the runbook' or 'a second rebar engineer reviews the runbook end-to-end and confirms no step requires consulting snap's repo.'
findings.finding: No AC carries a concrete validation mechanism for the beneficiary (rebar infra engineers). All four criteria are file-existence/content checks ('file X contains Y'), but none specify how an operator would confirm the docs are actually usable — e.g., that an oncall engineer can execute a rotation or revoke using only rebar's runbook without consulting snap's dead repo.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all four bullets)
findings.suggested_fix: Append an AC such as: '[ ] A rebar team member (not the author) performs a tabletop walkthrough of the cookie-key rotation section and confirms every SSM path, Lambda name, and AWS CLI command matches the deployed account state — any discrepancy is fixed before merge.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the `*.solutions.navateam.com` wildcard cert actually exists as an `aws_acm_certificate` in rebar's Terraform (currently absent) or is a Let's Encrypt cert managed via certbot (`infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template:45`), and correct the plan's ownership claim accordingly before writing AC1's subsection.
findings.finding: The plan asserts rebar owns 'the `*.solutions.navateam.com` wildcard cert,' but no `aws_acm_certificate` resource exists anywhere in `infra/terraform/`, and the existing nginx config uses a per-subdomain Let's Encrypt cert (`rebar.solutions.navateam.com/fullchain.pem`) — directly contradicting a wildcard ACM cert.
findings.location: What section — 'rebar now OWNS … the `*.solutions.navateam.com` wildcard cert'; AC1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before writing the runbook's subdomain-onboarding section, verify that story ample-famous-cockatiel has landed `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` (currently absent); if it has not, scope the runbook to use a placeholder path and note the dependency explicitly.
findings.finding: The What section states the runbook's 'future rebar subdomain' section references 'the reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module — which the terraform story adopts into rebar,' but no `infra/terraform/modules/` directory (let alone an `sso-gate` module) exists anywhere in the repo.
findings.location: What section — SSO operations runbook bullet, 'how a future rebar subdomain would opt into the SSO'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale in the SSO runbook bullet explaining WHY adapting snap's `auth/RUNBOOK.md` (rather than authoring from scratch) is the right approach — e.g. that it preserves tested rotation/revoke procedures and architectural context already validated in production.
findings.finding: The plan states the SSO runbook is 'adapted from snap's auth/RUNBOOK.md' but gives no positive rationale for why adaptation-over-fresh-authoring is the right approach. The positive case — that the snap runbook encodes tested operational knowledge (rotation procedures, incident revoke lever, architecture description) that should be preserved rather than re-derived — is the load-bearing justification for this choice, and its absence leaves the approach under-motivated.
findings.location: What section — SSO runbook bullet: 'adapted from snap's auth/RUNBOOK.md'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the SSO runbook bullet: 'Adapting rather than rewriting preserves the tested operational procedures (rotation steps, incident revoke sequence) that have been validated against the live stack, reducing the risk of introducing procedural errors in a freshly-authored document.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either remove the "future rebar subdomain opt-in" section from the runbook scope entirely (deferring it to a future ticket that owns that architecture), or obtain explicit parent/epic buy-in by adding it to the epic's scope before including it in this leaf.
findings.finding: The SSO operations runbook AC and What description include a forward-looking section on "how a future rebar subdomain would opt into the SSO," which goes beyond the parent story's and grandparent epic's declared scope of re-homing existing snap resources and adapting an existing runbook. Neither the parent (ample-famous-cockatiel, scope: terraform adoption + retag) nor the grandparent epic (scope: re-home snap shared AWS infra into rebar) mentions onboarding future subdomains as a deliverable.
findings.location: Leaf plan: What bullet 4 (

findings.finding: The plan's 'What' section asserts the SSO runbook will document 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module — which the terraform story adopts into rebar as code alongside the auth host.' This is a codebase existence claim: the module is described as existing ('reusable', 'an existing module') at plan-execution time. A directory listing of infra/terraform/ shows no `modules/` subdirectory, and a search for 'sso-gate' in infra/terraform returns zero hits. The module does not exist in the repo, and the Scope section only says this story 'Depends on the terraform adoption story landing (or landing alongside)' — it does not guarantee the module will exist when the doc story executes. If it hasn't landed yet, the runbook section it anchors is unbacked.
findings.location: What section — SSO runbook bullet; Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a hard dependency on the terraform adoption ticket in the Scope section and add an AC such as: 'The runbook's subdomain-protection section references `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` and the module exists at that path in the merged PR.' Alternatively, draft the runbook section with a clearly-marked `TODO: update path once terraform story lands` and exclude it from the runbook AC until then.

findings.finding: The What section asserts the SSO runbook will document 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module — which the terraform story adopts into rebar as code alongside the auth host.' However, no `infra/terraform/modules/` directory exists in the repo, and the sibling terraform story (7246-bd9e-58a3-41fd) explicitly states it 'Does NOT adopt snap's … audit-dashboard sso-gate.' The module will not exist when this story lands, making the runbook section's reference to an 'existing module' a hallucinated edit target.
findings.location: What section — SSO runbook bullet; 'infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate module'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the runbook's sso-gate module reference with a note that subdomain SSO gating via a reusable module is future work (not yet adopted), and document only what the terraform story actually delivers (auth_host, auth_sso, acm, oidc resources in infra/terraform/).

findings.finding: The SSO runbook (to be added under infra/runbooks/) references the reusable infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate module, but this module does not yet exist in the repository. The plan notes the terraform story is a dependency, but the runbook text contains no conditional guard — an operator reading the runbook before the terraform story lands will find a broken module reference with no warning.
findings.location: What section ('Add an SSO operations runbook under infra/runbooks/') — 'the reusable infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate module — which the terraform story adopts into rebar as code'

findings.finding: The plan asserts the SSO runbook will document 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module — which the terraform story adopts into rebar as code alongside the auth host, so the runbook references an existing module.' No `infra/terraform/modules/` directory exists anywhere in the repo. The terraform adoption story is listed as an unconfirmed dependency ('Depends on the terraform adoption story landing or landing alongside'). If this doc story merges before — or independently of — the terraform story, the runbook will reference a module that does not exist, producing broken/misleading documentation for the security-critical SSO onboarding path.
findings.location: What section — SSO runbook bullet; Scope — dependency clause
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit merge-ordering gate in the Scope: 'This story MUST NOT merge before the terraform adoption story lands (sso-gate module required).' Alternatively, scope the sso-gate section of the runbook to a stub with a forward-reference: 'The `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module is being adopted in [terraform story link]; this section will be completed once that story lands.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts the SSO runbook will reference 'an existing module' — specifically `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` — but no `infra/terraform/modules/` directory exists in the repo and no `sso-gate` reference appears anywhere in the codebase. This makes the runbook's 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via sso-gate' section unwritable as specified unless the module is created first (which the plan scopes to the terraform adoption story, not this doc story).
findings.location: What section — SSO runbook bullet: 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module — which the terraform story adopts into rebar as code alongside the auth host, so the runbook references an existing module'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit sequencing constraint: 'This story MUST NOT merge until the terraform adoption story (which creates infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate) is merged.' Alternatively, stub the runbook section with a TODO referencing the terraform story's ticket, so the doc is honest about the module's pending state.

findings.finding: The plan states the SSO runbook will document 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module — which the terraform story adopts into rebar as code alongside the auth host.' But `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` does not exist in the repo, and the sibling terraform story (7246-bd9e-58a3-41fd) explicitly excludes it: 'Does NOT adopt snap's github_deploy role, audit-dashboard sso-gate.' The plan's assertion that sso-gate will exist at implementation time is contradicted by the dependency ticket's stated scope.
findings.location: What section — SSO runbook bullet: 'infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate module — which the terraform story adopts into rebar as code alongside the auth host'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the sso-gate runbook section with a description of how the auth_host + API GW proxy pattern (as actually adopted in auth_host.tf/auth_sso.tf) can be extended to protect a new subdomain, since that is what the terraform story delivers.

findings.finding: The What section asserts the SSO runbook will reference 'an existing module' at infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate, but no such module exists anywhere in the repo (infra/terraform/ has no modules/ subdirectory; all sso-gate searches return zero hits). The Scope clause hedges with 'Depends on the terraform adoption story landing (or landing alongside)', but the parenthetical '— which the terraform story adopts into rebar as code alongside the auth host, so the runbook references an existing module' treats the module's existence as a resolved fact. This is an unverified capability assertion: if the terraform story hasn't landed, the runbook section describing how to use sso-gate will reference a non-existent artifact.
findings.location: What section, SSO operations runbook bullet: '...how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate module — which the terraform story adopts into rebar as code alongside the auth host, so the runbook references an existing module'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the runbook bullet to: 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module (at infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate, adopted in the companion terraform story — verify that story has merged before following this section)' and add an AC: 'The SSO runbook's sso-gate section is gated on / validated against the actual module landing in infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate.'

findings.finding: The runbook AC requires documenting 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module', but no `sso-gate` module exists anywhere in the repo (searches for both `sso-gate` and `sso_gate` return zero hits). The runbook will either document a nonexistent module or the AC silently depends on that module being created/adopted in the companion terraform story — neither case is addressed in this plan.
findings.location: What section — SSO operations runbook bullet; AC line 4 ('An `infra/runbooks/` SSO runbook covers architecture, secrets, rotation, and incident revoke')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'The SSO runbook's sso-gate section is gated on the sso-gate module landing in the terraform adoption story; stub that section with a TODO if the module is not yet merged.' Alternatively, if sso-gate is a snap artifact being adopted, name it explicitly and confirm its HCL path.

findings.finding: The 'What' section specifies the SSO runbook should cover 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module', but no sso-gate module exists anywhere in the repo, and the parent terraform adoption ticket (7246) explicitly excludes it: 'Does NOT adopt snap's ... audit-dashboard sso-gate'. The runbook would be documenting a non-existent module.
findings.location: What section, SSO runbook bullet: 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module' with 'how to add a new rebar subdomain to the existing auth_host CloudFront/Lambda SSO stack' — or remove the bullet entirely if that procedure is out of scope for this docs ticket.

findings.finding: The runbook's 'What' section directs documenting 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module' and asserts it is 'an existing module,' but no such module exists anywhere in the repo: `infra/terraform/` has no `modules/` subdirectory and a full-repo search for 'sso-gate' returns zero hits in any source file. The plan acknowledges this is contingent on 'the terraform adoption story landing (or landing alongside).' Authoring a runbook section that treats a non-existent, zero-call-site module as a stable, reusable abstraction is a premature-abstraction / YAGNI violation within the documentation scope.
findings.location: What section — 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module — which the terraform story adopts into rebar as code alongside the auth host, so the runbook references an existing module'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module — which the terraform story adopts into rebar as code alongside the auth host, so the runbook references an existing module' with a conditional note: 'Once the SSO terraform adoption story lands, a reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module will be available; use it to protect a new subdomain as follows: …' This makes the runbook honest about ordering and avoids asserting a non-existent abstraction.

findings.finding: The leaf's runbook 'What' promises to document 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate module — which the terraform story adopts into rebar as code alongside the auth host.' The sibling terraform story (7246) explicitly excluded the sso-gate: its Scope says 'Does NOT adopt snap's … audit-dashboard sso-gate', and the operator-attested plan/apply comment confirms it was intentionally dropped. The parent epic (d58f) also does not include sso-gate adoption. The leaf is documenting a module that the parent's plan does not contain — and that the sibling terraform story explicitly ruled out.
findings.location: Leaf 'What' section — runbook bullet: 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate module — which the terraform story adopts into rebar as code alongside the auth host'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the sso-gate subdomain-protection section from the runbook's 'What' bullet. The runbook should cover only the adopted SSO stack (architecture, secrets at /auth-solutions/*, cookie-key rotation, Google-secret rotation, incident revoke lever) — all of which are in scope per the parent. If sso-gate documentation is genuinely wanted, first add sso-gate adoption to the parent epic and the terraform sibling, then re-scope this leaf.

findings.finding: The 'What' section asserts the SSO runbook will document 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate module — which the terraform story adopts into rebar as code alongside the auth host, so the runbook references an existing module.' No infra/terraform/modules/ directory exists in the repo and no sso-gate module is present anywhere, meaning the 'existing module' claim is currently Contradicted. If the companion terraform story has not landed when this doc story executes, the author cannot accurately document the module's inputs, outputs, and usage pattern because the artifact to reference does not exist.
findings.location: What section — SSO runbook bullet; Scope: 'Depends on the terraform adoption story landing (or landing alongside)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Scope line to: 'Documentation only. The SSO runbook's subdomain-protection section MUST be written after the terraform adoption story lands and infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate/ is present in the repo; this story is a hard dependent of that terraform story, not merely an optional co-lander.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
